summary: Joel announces the launch of job boards on Stack Overflow and Server Fault, linked with the existing Joel on Software job board, to give employers a wider audience of quality developers. He notes that Stack Overflow's community of 3.7 million monthly visitors already serves as an informal recruiting tool, since employers can evaluate candidates by reading their technical answers. Joel argues that Stack Overflow reputation and tag-specific expertise are far more useful hiring signals than traditional resumes. He and Jeff Atwood plan to build features that formalize this connection between demonstrated expertise and hiring.
key_points: The Joel on Software job board is being expanded by linking with new Stack Overflow and Server Fault job boards, tripling employer reach
Stack Overflow already functions as an informal recruiting platform because candidates' technical abilities are visible through their answers
Traditional resumes provide the wrong information about candidates compared to demonstrated expertise on specific technical topics
Developers are already putting Stack Overflow reputation scores on their resumes, signaling a shift in how technical competence is evaluated
money_quotes: I'm pretty sure we're the only job listing service in the world with an unconditional money back guarantee.
Stack Overflow is already a great place to find good programmers, because you can see how good people really are by reading the answers that they post.

If you wanted to hire an iPhone developer, would you rather know that person's Stack Overflow stats in the iPhone tag and read their answers to technical questions? Or would you rather know where they went to college?
I've always hated traditional resumes, which just don't give the right kind of information about a candidate.
If we pull this off, getting jobs in the tech industry will be a lot saner.
key_insight: Demonstrated expertise through public technical contributions is a far better hiring signal than traditional resumes and credentials.
